metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to 978d's AC a requirement to verify the pinned json-repair library version supports `schema=<model>` and cite the version/docs; if unavailable, fall back to the existing call signature.
findings.finding: 978d changes the `repair_json` call in `structured.py` to add `schema=<model>` but does not confirm that the `json-repair` library version pinned in the project exposes a `schema=` parameter, nor does it identify `json-repair` as a consumer whose interface must be verified.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc: What section (Output self-heal), AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: In 978d's What section, add: 'Confirm json-repair >= X.Y supports schema= (cite library docs/changelog); pin that minimum version in pyproject.toml's json-repair dependency if not already met.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Record the spike (story 95a5) output — specifically the printed result of `python -c "import importlib.metadata as m; print('retries' in m.metadata('pydantic-ai-slim').get_all('Provides-Extra'))"` and `python -c "from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(AsyncTenacityTransport.__init__))"`— as a concrete CI-reproducible artifact in the Architecture constraints section, AND name a fallback design (e.g., a custom `httpx.AsyncTransport` subclass with tenacity) to be executed if the spike is negative.
findings.finding: `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and the `[retries]` extra are the foundational dependency for all 7 child stories, but the plan's verification command cannot be confirmed in this environment, no codebase file imports or depends on `pydantic_ai.retries`, and three prior review passes independently flagged the extra as nonexistent. The plan dismisses those findings as 'code-blind' but provides only a self-asserted probe — no output, no CI artifact, no pinned release note — as evidence. The spike (story 95a5) is the stated de-risk gate, but the plan commits the full 7-story architecture before the spike result exists, with no named fallback if the spike is negative.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC bullet: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Architecture constraints section: a pinned spike-output block showing the exact probe results on pydantic-ai-slim==1.107.0, plus a one-paragraph fallback: 'If `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` is unavailable, the transport-retry layer will be implemented as a direct `httpx.AsyncTransport` subclass wrapping tenacity, injected the same way via `http_client=`.' This eliminates the single-point-of-failure on an unverified symbol.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture' to 2eca's Acceptance Criteria ADR list, matching the parent AC13 requirement.
findings.finding: 2eca's own Acceptance Criteria omits the 'transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture' ADR, even though the parent AC13 requires all four ADRs (exit-11, diagnostic-sanitization, deferred async watchdog, AND transport-retry side-effect-safety) to be recorded by 2eca as sole ADR owner.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38: Acceptance Criteria (ADRs bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: In 2eca's AC, change the ADRs bullet to: 'ADRs recorded (exit-11 contract, diagnostic sanitization, deferred async watchdog, transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture)' — four ADRs, matching parent AC13 exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the deploy sequence for exit-11 consumers (e.g., 'update CI scripts and Gerrit bot to handle exit 11 before or atomically with the rebar release') and add a gate-wiring story AC line that confirms consumer updates are shipped as part of the same release or are verified additive-safe by inspection.
findings.finding: The plan identifies CI and the Gerrit reviewer-bot as code-specific exit-code consumers that need updating for exit 11, but does not specify the deploy sequence: whether those consumers must be updated before or after the new rebar binary ships. During the gap window (new rebar deployed, consumers not yet updated), any gate returning exit 11 would be misinterpreted by the Gerrit bot as an unexpected code (potentially treated as unrecognized/error rather than retryable-INDETERMINATE).
findings.location: Architecture constraints section: 'enumerate external consumers (CI, the Gerrit reviewer-bot pipeline) — owned by the gate-wiring story. Additive (any-non-zero-is-failure consumers unaffected; only code-specific special-casers update)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the gate-wiring story's AC: 'CI job definitions and the Gerrit reviewer-bot are updated to handle exit 11 (treat as retryable-INDETERMINATE) and this update is deployed before or simultaneously with the rebar binary that can emit exit 11; the PR description records the deploy order.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a reproducible symbol-level verification command for `wait_retry_after` (parallel to the `AsyncTenacityTransport` probe) that confirms its exact name, signature, and how it composes with `AsyncTenacityTransport` at pydantic-ai-slim 1.107.0, and record the result in the Architecture constraints section.
findings.finding: The `wait_retry_after` symbol is asserted as a usable named export from `pydantic_ai.retries` (the mechanism that makes 'Retry-After honored and capped' work), but unlike `AsyncTenacityTransport` no verification command resolves its exact name or signature. Three prior review passes already misidentified the entire module as nonexistent, and the repo contains zero usage of this symbol anywhere. If the callable name, signature, or integration pattern differs from what the plan assumes, the Retry-After-aware backoff AC is silently unimplemented.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point' paragraph; AC: 'Retry-After is honored and capped'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Architecture constraints block: `python -c "from pydantic_ai.retries import wait_retry_after; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(wait_retry_after))"` — if this fails, try `python -c "import pydantic_ai.retries as r; print(dir(r))"` to discover the actual retry-after helper name, and update the plan to use the verified symbol.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a symbol-level verification command for `wait_retry_after` (parallel to the `AsyncTenacityTransport` check) in the Architecture constraints section — e.g., `python -c "from pydantic_ai.retries import wait_retry_after; import inspect; print(inspect.signature(wait_retry_after))"` — and record the confirmed name/signature.
findings.finding: `wait_retry_after` is asserted as a named export of `pydantic_ai.retries` and is the mechanism by which `Retry-After` header honoring is implemented, but unlike `AsyncTenacityTransport` no verification command is provided for this specific symbol. If the actual callable name or signature differs (e.g., it is a constructor argument to `AsyncTenacityTransport` rather than a standalone function), the Retry-After AC ('honored and capped') is silently unimplemented.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC bullet: 'Retry-After is honored and capped'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the Architecture constraints 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet to explicitly state that the sanitizer must be *implemented and tested*, not only documented in an ADR, aligning it with AC bullet 10.
findings.finding: Architecture constraints section says 'Diagnostic sanitization...recorded as an ADR,' implying documentation-only treatment, while the AC explicitly contradicts this: 'The diagnostic sanitizer is implemented AND tested (a planted secret is scrubbed before any sink) — not merely recorded in an ADR.' An implementer reading the Architecture constraints section would conclude an ADR entry suffices; the AC says the opposite.
findings.location: Architecture constraints ('Diagnostic sanitization' bullet) vs AC bullet 10
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Architecture constraints bullet to: 'Diagnostic sanitization. The verbatim diagnostic is scrubbed of secrets/credentials/PII before any sink (logs, CLI stderr, session log, verdict); the scrubbing logic is implemented in code and tested (a planted secret must not reach any sink). The decision and contract are recorded in an ADR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the exact import path and constructor/sanitizer signatures for `LLMOutcome` and the sanitization helper in 30ff's AC or scope, and have 978d reference that pinned interface explicitly.
findings.finding: 978d calls 30ff's `LLMOutcome` constructor and sanitizer as an inter-child public API boundary, but neither child specifies the module path, method signature, or stability contract for that interface — creating a silent-breakage risk if 30ff's module layout changes between wave 1 and wave 2.
findings.location: child 978d-1d5d-8287-47bc: What section (Refusal-as-content protocol); child 30ff-21a3-0724-422b: Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 30ff's scope: 'Exports `LLMOutcome` and `build_llm_outcome(resolution_class, exc, ctx) -> LLMOutcome` from `src/rebar/llm/classifier.py` (or `errors.py`) as a stable public interface for wave-2 consumers.' Then have 978d reference that exact import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] List all 7 child story titles (and IDs/links if available) directly in this epic plan so AC-1 is verifiable without external tracker access.
findings.finding: The plan's first and governing AC says 'All 7 child stories are delivered' but the 7 child stories are never listed, named, or linked within this document. An executor reading only this epic plan cannot identify what those stories are, verify they exist, or confirm the count is correct.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 1: 'All 7 child stories are delivered, each passing its own acceptance criteria and CI'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Child Stories' section enumerating all 7 stories with title, ID, and one-line scope summary (e.g., '95a5 — Transport-injection spike', 'XXX — Classification/disposition enum', 'XXX — Gate wiring + exit-11', etc.).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'de-risked by a spike' with a concrete artifact: e.g., 'proven by a passing integration test in story 95a5 that injects a 429→200 sequence through the custom transport and observes retry without tool re-execution'.
findings.finding: The AC 'Transport-injection integration point is de-risked by a spike' uses the subjective term 'de-risked' with no stated observable artifact — a reviewer cannot determine in-session whether this criterion is met.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 8: 'The transport-injection integration point is de-risked by a spike before dependent stories build on it'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'The transport-injection spike (story 95a5) is complete when a passing automated test demonstrates: (a) a custom AsyncTenacityTransport is forwarded to the Anthropic SDK under agent.run_sync(), and (b) a simulated 429→200 sequence retries at the transport layer without re-invoking already-executed tools.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the required artifact for 'confirmed additive-safe': e.g., a section in docs/exit-codes.md listing each consumer and its handling, OR a comment block in the pinning test enumerating them, so the criterion is checkable via repo diff.
findings.finding: The AC 'exit-code consumers enumerated + confirmed additive-safe' has no stated artifact or definition of done for 'confirmed' — it is unclear what evidence satisfies this criterion in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 9 / Architecture constraints: 'enumerate external consumers (CI, the Gerrit reviewer-bot pipeline) — owned by the gate-wiring story'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the gate-wiring story's AC: 'A doc section (or inline comment in test_exit_codes.py) lists each identified consumer of exit codes (CI workflow files, Gerrit reviewer-bot config) and states whether they use any-non-zero or code-specific branching; the gate-wiring PR diff must include this artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a bullet to Architecture constraints (or the gate-wiring story description) explicitly describing the content and rationale for the 'transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture' ADR, so it has covered described work matching the AC.
findings.finding: The AC bullet for ADRs lists four required ADRs including 'transport-retry side-effect-safety architecture,' but the Architecture constraints section — which enumerates ADR topics — lists only three (exit-code signal, diagnostic sanitization, async watchdog). The fourth ADR has no described work in the plan body, making it an orphan AC criterion.
findings.location: Architecture constraints ('Exit-code signal', 'Diagnostic sanitization', async watchdog bullets) vs AC final bullet listing 4 ADRs
findings.suggested_fix: Add under Architecture constraints: 'Transport-retry side-effect-safety (recorded as an ADR): documents why retry at the httpx transport layer is safe for the three gate ops (no already-executed tool side effects are duplicated), the invariant that SDK max_retries=0 enforces single-layer ownership, and the conditions under which this guarantee could break (e.g., if a tool is promoted above the transport boundary). Owned by the gate-wiring story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Standardize terminology: explicitly state in one place (e.g., Architecture constraints) that `resolution_class` is the Python attribute on the verdict object whose type is the `ResolutionDisposition` enum, and use that pairing consistently throughout the plan.
findings.finding: Terminology drift: the classification concept is called 'resolution-disposition set' and 'resolution-disposition enum' in Context/AC, but 'resolution_class' (snake_case attribute name) in Architecture constraints. While one may be a Python identifier and the other a concept label, the plan never explicitly maps the two names, creating ambiguity about whether they refer to the same entity.
findings.location: Context ('closed resolution-disposition set'), Architecture constraints ('bifurcated by the resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage'), AC ('closed resolution-disposition enum')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical in Architecture constraints: 'bifurcated by the `resolution_class` field (type: `ResolutionDisposition` enum, the closed resolution-disposition set defined in Context)' — and use `ResolutionDisposition` wherever the enum type is named.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a story-map table or list to the plan body naming all 7 child stories with a one-line scope summary each, so AC bullet 1 is verifiable and every described deliverable maps to at least one named story.
findings.finding: The AC criterion 'All 7 child stories are delivered' measures 7 deliverables, but the plan body never enumerates or names more than one child story (story 95a5, the spike). An implementer or reviewer cannot verify this AC criterion against the plan because 6 of the 7 stories are undescribed here.
findings.location: AC bullet 1 ('All 7 child stories are delivered') vs entire plan body

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate verdict-schema consumers (CI, Gerrit reviewer-bot, any archival/reporting pipelines), confirm the `resolution_class` addition is additive-only for all of them, and add a one-line rollback path (e.g., make the field optional with a default) to the gate-wiring story.
findings.finding: The verdict schema is being modified (adding `resolution_class` to the coverage/verdict output) but the plan does not provide an expand-contract sequence or rollback path for external consumers of that schema (CI pipelines, the Gerrit reviewer-bot are named consumers of exit codes but not of verdict schema fields). If a consumer parses verdict JSON today, a new required field or a changed shape is a breaking change with no stated migration.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; AC — 'verdict-schema edits'; Success Criteria — 'never produce a signed/certified verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `resolution_class` as an optional field with a sentinel default (e.g., `None` or `'NEEDS_INVESTIGATION'`) so existing consumers that don't read it are unaffected; document this in the verdict schema changelog and add a migration note to the gate-wiring story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the `max_retries=0` change: state that if the transport-retry feature is disabled or unavailable, `max_retries` must be restored to its SDK default (2), and document this in the transport-retry ADR.
findings.finding: The `max_retries=0` SDK change removes the Anthropic SDK's existing 2-retry safety net. The startup guard prevents a *silent* regression, but no explicit rollback path is stated for reverting this behavioral change if the transport-retry approach needs to be abandoned (e.g., if the spike fails, or if `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is removed from the package in a future version).
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC — 'SDK `max_retries=0`'; AC — 'startup guard that fails fast'
findings.suggested_fix: In the transport-retry ADR, add a section: 'Rollback: set `AnthropicProvider(max_retries=2)` and remove `AsyncTenacityTransport` injection; re-enable with `verify.llm_retry_max_attempts` > 0 once transport is confirmed working.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC line requiring the verdict schema reference documentation to be updated (or created if absent) to reflect the `resolution_class` field addition, owned by the gate-wiring story alongside the other schema edits.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new config keys (`verify.llm_retry_max_attempts`, request/tool timeouts, step caps) and a new verdict field (`resolution_class`) but does not explicitly call out whether the existing verdict schema documentation or any schema reference docs become stale — the plan's doc AC covers taxonomy/retry/timeout/config but does not mention updating or versioning the verdict schema reference.
findings.location: AC — 'Docs cover the taxonomy, the retry/timeout model, the `run_sync`-compatible liveness design, and the config keys'; Architecture constraints — 'verdict-schema edits'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a calibration note or small measurement spike (e.g., sample of existing gate-op run logs) to validate the step-cap and timeout defaults before they are committed as the shipped starting point, or explicitly mark them as 'provisional — revisit after first production runs' in the config doc.
findings.finding: Config defaults (retry attempts=4, wait-cap=60s, request timeout=600s, tool timeout=120s, step caps=60 requests/200 tool-calls) are asserted as fixed starting values with no measurement or calibration plan — no spike, fixture, or 'TBD: derive from X' note exists to validate that these values work for the range of expected agentic run profiles (small-context fast runs through large-context multi-tool runs).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Config keys ship concrete defaults' bullet; Architecture constraints — 'run_sync-compatible activity-based liveness' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate-wiring story's config-doc section, annotate each default with its derivation basis (e.g., 'based on observed p95 of N runs' or 'conservative starting point — revisit after 2-week production sampling') and add a follow-on ticket to recalibrate from real run telemetry after initial rollout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the gate-wiring story (or its ADR) that sanitization occurs at a single choke point — the classifier output, before the `ErrorClassification` / diagnostic object is returned to any caller — so all downstream sinks receive an already-scrubbed string by construction, rather than relying on each sink to call the sanitizer independently.
findings.finding: The diagnostic sanitizer is specified to scrub secrets/credentials/PII 'before any sink (logs, CLI stderr, session log, verdict)' but the plan does not specify the integration boundary — whether sanitization happens at a single central point (e.g., the classifier output) or must be applied independently at each sink. Without this, child stories may implement per-sink scrubbing inconsistently, leaving some sinks unscrubbed.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — 'The diagnostic sanitizer is implemented AND tested' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Define the sanitizer as a post-init validator or factory-method wrapper on the `ErrorClassification` dataclass itself (or equivalent), so the scrubbed diagnostic is the only public representation of the object. Document this boundary in the diagnostic-sanitization ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit classifier rule (HTTP status code(s) and/or Anthropic error-body fields) that identifies budget-exhaustion as non-retryable and maps it to INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS/CHANGE_SETTINGS, and add a corresponding AC line requiring a RED test that verifies no retry occurs on a budget-exhausted response.
findings.finding: The plan titles 'budget' as a first-class failure mode alongside rate-limits and outages, but the body never specifies how budget-exhaustion errors (e.g., a permanent HTTP 402 / credit-depleted response from Anthropic) are distinguished from transient overload 529s that share the same retry path. Without this distinction, a budget-exhausted account would be endlessly retried until the retry cap is hit rather than immediately classified as CHANGE_SETTINGS/INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS and surfaced as non-retryable.
findings.location: Title + Architecture constraints section ('529 added explicitly; Retry-After honored and capped') + Acceptance Criteria ('529 is retried')
findings.suggested_fix: In the classifier story, enumerate Anthropic's credit-exhaustion error shape (e.g., HTTP 402 or a 429 with `error.type == 'insufficient_quota'`) as a non-retryable case; the `AsyncTenacityTransport` retry predicate should explicitly exclude this status/body so it propagates immediately to the classifier, which routes it to INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS with a diagnostic message naming the budget issue.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a shared `_make_anthropic_provider(base_url=..., http_client=...)` factory (or equivalent) that BOTH the normal and loopback-bypass AnthropicProvider construction paths call through, so neither can be independently mis-wired; the gate-wiring story should name this factory as a required design element.
findings.finding: The plan requires transport retry on EVERY Anthropic model-construction path, including the loopback-proxy-bypass path, but provides no shared factory mechanism to guarantee this. The loopback-bypass path (runner.py:233-240) constructs AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct) inline without http_client=. If the gate-wiring story wires AsyncTenacityTransport only at the default construction site and misses this 6-line inline block, that path gets max_retries=0 with no transport retry — worse than the current 2-retry baseline.
findings.location: Architecture constraints / AC: 'Transport retry + max_retries=0 apply on EVERY Anthropic model-construction path (normal model-string AND loopback-proxy-bypass)'; runner.py:233-240 (_local_proxy_bypass_base_url path)
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a `_anthropic_provider_with_transport(base_url: str | None = None) -> AnthropicProvider` helper that always wires the `AsyncTenacityTransport` as `http_client=` and accepts an optional `base_url`. Both the normal path and the loopback-bypass path (runner.py:240) call this helper, making it impossible to wire one without the other.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite ADR-0036 as prior art in the Architecture constraints section, explain whether the transport-retry backoff policy (cap, jitter formula, Retry-After parsing) is intentionally consistent with or intentionally different from ADR-0036's policy, and note whether this story partially addresses the janitor's retry-consolidation recommendation.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new Retry-After-aware, jitter-bounded LLM backoff mechanism (via `wait_retry_after`) without citing ADR-0036 (`0036-acli-429-rate-limit-backoff.md`), which established the same pattern — bounded, Retry-After-aware, jittered exponential — for ACLI 429s in this same repo. The design decisions (cap at 60s, honor Retry-After iff present, jittered fallback) are duplicated rather than drawn from a shared policy. This is the seventh independent retry/backoff implementation flagged by the repo's own janitor report as architectural decay.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Self-healing retry at the httpx transport layer' bullet; Architecture constraints — 'Retry-After honored and capped'; Config defaults AC: 'wait-cap 60s'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the Architecture constraints 'injection point' description to explicitly address the loopback-proxy-bypass path (runner.py:233–240), naming the concrete mechanism (e.g., 'the same `AsyncTenacityTransport`-wrapped `http_client` is passed to both `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct, http_client=...)` and the normal path') and requiring the spike (story 95a5) to validate BOTH construction paths.
findings.finding: The Architecture constraints section describes a single `AnthropicProvider` injection point, but the codebase has two independent `AnthropicProvider` construction paths: (1) the normal model-string path and (2) the loopback-proxy-bypass path at runner.py:233–240 which constructs `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` independently. The Architecture constraints section's injection description does not address this second path. While AC bullet 11 does assert 'Transport retry + `max_retries=0` apply on EVERY Anthropic model-construction path (normal model-string AND loopback-proxy-bypass)', no implementation guidance is given for HOW the second path is covered, leaving a gap that the spike (story 95a5) may not exercise if it only validates the normal path.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; runner.py:233–240; AC bullet 11

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the concrete pattern classes the diagnostic sanitizer MUST cover (at minimum: Anthropic API key regex `sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9-]+`, auth header values, account/org identifiers, and echoed request-body secrets), and add test cases for each pattern class — not just one planted secret — to the sanitizer acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The diagnostic sanitizer's coverage scope is underdeclared: the AC requires only that 'a planted secret is scrubbed before any sink,' but does not define which secret/credential/PII *patterns* are in scope. Verbatim API error diagnostics captured from the Anthropic boundary can contain API key prefixes (e.g. `sk-ant-…`), account/org identifiers, or echoed request-body fragments — none of which are enumerated. A narrowly implemented sanitizer could pass the single-pattern test while leaking these through to the verdict (which flows to git history / CI logs).
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC line: 'The diagnostic sanitizer is implemented AND tested (a planted secret is scrubbed before any sink)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the sanitizer AC to enumerate pattern classes and require one test per class: (1) Anthropic key regex (`sk-ant-…`), (2) generic Bearer/auth header value, (3) any string matching the env-var name used for the API key, (4) echoed request fragments above a configurable length threshold. A `pytest.mark.parametrize` table covering these classes makes the requirement unambiguous and prevents a narrow implementation from silently regressing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a mapping table (exception type / HTTP status / condition → resolution-disposition enum value) co-located with the enum definition, covering at minimum: 429 → WAIT_AND_RETRY, 529 → WAIT_AND_RETRY, 5xx → RETRY_NOW, auth/401/403 → CHANGE_SETTINGS, 402 → INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, context-length → CHANGE_INPUT, UsageLimitExceeded → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION.
findings.finding: The resolution-disposition enum's 8 values (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, CHANGE_SETTINGS, CHANGE_INPUT, CHANGE_PROVIDER_OR_MODEL, INCREASE_PROVIDER_LIMITS, FIX_AGENT_DESIGN, NEEDS_INVESTIGATION) are named but the mapping protocol — which exception type, HTTP status code, or error signal routes to which enum value — is never specified anywhere in the plan. An executor implementing the classifier has no authoritative table to code from.
findings.location: Settled design decisions bullet: 'Classify what survives retries into a closed resolution-disposition set'; AC: 'Every LLM failure is classified into the closed resolution-disposition enum + verbatim diagnostic; the classifier is total'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Classifier mapping table' subsection under the settled-design bullet with a two-column table: condition (exception class or HTTP status range) → resolution-disposition value + whether it is in the retry set. This is the decision protocol an implementor needs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `resolution_class` (or equivalent) in the verdict schema(s) — at minimum as a named property on `coverage` with an explicit enum of the allowed values and document which layer (classifier → gate-wiring → CLI) is responsible for writing vs. reading it.
findings.finding: `resolution_class` is stated as the field on verdict `coverage` that bifurcates exit 11 from exit 2, but this field does not exist in any of the three verdict schemas (plan_review_verdict.schema.json, code_review_verdict.schema.json, completion_verdict.schema.json — all confirmed by grep), and the plan never specifies which layer writes it (the transport classifier, the gate-wiring story, or the CLI exit-code mapper) nor what its allowed values are.
findings.location: Architecture constraints: 'Exit-code signal. Exit 11 vs the existing INDETERMINATE exit 2 is bifurcated by the `resolution_class` carried on the verdict `coverage`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each named silent-success detection mechanism (refusal-as-content, schema-shape check, truncated-looks-complete), specify the post-detection fallback: which exception class is raised (or which verdict state is set), and whether it enters the retry loop or fast-fails.
findings.finding: The silent-success detection sub-steps (refusal-as-content detection, verdict schema-shape check, truncated-looks-complete detection) each have no specified fallback: the plan names them as detectors but never states what happens after a positive detection in each gate — whether it routes to INDETERMINATE verdict, raises LLMUnavailableError, triggers a re-ask, or results in exit 11.
findings.location: Settled design decisions bullet: 'Silent-success layer: the per-request/per-tool timeout as the silent-hang backstop, a verdict schema-shape check, structured refusal-as-content detection…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit coordination protocol for concurrent wave-2 config.py edits: either 2eca pre-defines the config namespace stubs in a wave-0 commit that 95a5 and fd1d fill in, or name one of the two wave-2 children as the config-file merge owner with the other submitting a follow-on.
findings.finding: Wave-2 parallel children 95a5 and fd1d both write to `config.py`/`_config_schema.py` with no explicit merge-coordination protocol, while 2eca (the stated config-namespace single-source-of-truth) lands in wave 3 — after both edits have already been made.
findings.location: child 95a5-41eb-cffd-477b and child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4: Scope/Files sections; child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38: Integration ownership
findings.suggested_fix: Have 2eca (or a pre-wave-2 commit) add placeholder config-schema stubs with agreed types/defaults for all `verify.llm_*` keys before wave 2 merges, so 95a5 and fd1d fill in values rather than defining the schema independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in 2eca whether gate_dispatch.py is an existing file, a new file being created, or a misreference to an existing gate-wiring location in runner.py or _llm_commands.py; update the file list and notify dependent children accordingly.
findings.finding: `gate_dispatch.py` is listed as a primary file in 2eca's scope/files but is not mentioned, created, or referenced by any other child — it is either a phantom file (does not exist in the codebase) or a new file whose creation and interface are never announced to dependent children.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38: Files section ('src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py')
findings.suggested_fix: If gate_dispatch.py is a new module, add a note to 2eca's What section describing its role and interface, and add a cross-reference in a879's scope so the RED baseline tests cover the dispatch path. If it's a misreference, correct the file path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC checklist item to fd1d: '95a5's transport-injection spike is green (confirmed) before this story merges its shared-client timeout contribution.'
findings.finding: fd1d's intra-wave dependency on 95a5's spike is stated in 2eca's wave notes but is NOT reflected as a hard prerequisite in fd1d's own Acceptance Criteria or scope — meaning fd1d could be approved and merged without confirming the spike passed.
findings.location: child fd1d-5f0d-4bd8-40f4: Acceptance Criteria and Scope; child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38: Integration ownership (wave 2 note)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to fd1d's AC: '- [ ] 95a5's spike integration test passes (transport injection confirmed viable) as a prerequisite gate before merging the shared-client timeout contribution.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate session-log consumers (CLI log display, CI artifact upload, external log aggregators) in 2eca's consumer-impact analysis and confirm the new diagnostic write is additive-safe for each.
findings.finding: Session-log readers are not enumerated in the consumer-impact analysis despite 2eca introducing a new structured `diagnostic` write to the session log — an incomplete consumer enumeration for the consumer-impact cross-child mode.
findings.location: child 2eca-0a1d-45c2-4e38: What section (Session-log sink ownership)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Session-log consumers' subsection to 2eca's consumer-impact analysis, listing known readers of `log_session`/`append_session_log` output and confirming the diagnostic field is either: (a) appended as a clearly-delimited new entry type, or (b) added to an existing structured entry in a backward-compatible way.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
